planting.note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed; grows quickly in warm soil.

directions: Broadcast buckwheat seed onto worked soil once frost danger has passed and rake in lightly for good contact. It germinates within a week and races to flower in about a month, smothering weeds and drawing in bees. Mow or turn it under at first bloom to add green matter and free up phosphorus for the next crop. Succession-sow every few weeks through summer for continuous soil coverage, but never let a stand fully set seed unless you want volunteers.

planting.note: Sow in early spring or late summer/fall for a winter-killed cover crop in cold zones.

directions: Broadcast oats densely over prepared ground in early spring or six to eight weeks before your first fall frost and rake in about half an inch deep. They establish fast in cool weather, forming a thick mat that outcompetes weeds and holds soil in place. In cold climates a hard freeze winter-kills the stand, leaving an easy-to-plant mulch by spring. You can also cut young growth for cat grass or graze it before it heads out.

planting.note: Sow winter wheat in fall for overwintering or spring wheat in early spring.

directions: Sow wheat by broadcasting or drilling into prepared soil about one inch deep, either in autumn for an overwintering cover or in early spring. Winter wheat establishes roots before dormancy, protecting soil through the cold months and greening up quickly when temperatures rise. As a cover crop, turn it under before it heads out to add organic matter, or let it ripen to a golden head for grain, wheatgrass, or microgreens. Keep evenly moist during establishment for a uniform stand.

planting.note: Fall or early spring sowing suits this cool-season native grass; benefits from cold stratification.

directions: Sow Canada wild rye in fall or early spring, pressing seed into contact with bare soil no more than a quarter inch deep. This fast-establishing native cool-season grass is a favorite nurse crop, holding slopes against erosion while slower prairie species take hold. Its arching, feathery seed heads feed birds and add graceful movement to naturalized plantings. Water during establishment, then let it fend for itself as a hardy, largely maintenance-free perennial.

planting.note: Sow late summer to early fall, or early spring in cold climates.

directions: Broadcast crimson clover onto firmed soil and rake in shallowly, timing sowing for late summer or early autumn so plants establish before winter. This annual legume fixes atmospheric nitrogen while its deep red, elongated blooms feed early pollinators the following spring. Turn the stand under at peak bloom for maximum nitrogen return before it sets seed. It also makes a striking, low-maintenance living mulch between rows.

planting.note: Sow in spring or early fall; a hardy perennial ground cover.

directions: Scatter Dutch white clover thinly over raked soil in spring or early fall and press in for good seed-to-soil contact, keeping the surface moist until it sprouts. This low-growing perennial legume spreads by creeping stems to form a durable living ground cover that fixes nitrogen and crowds out weeds. It thrives as a lawn companion, orchard understory, or pathway cover and tolerates light foot traffic. Mow occasionally to keep it tidy and encourage dense growth.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost when soil is warm; amaranth loves heat.

directions: Direct sow green garnet amaranth after the last frost into warm soil, barely covering the tiny seeds. Thin plants for grain heads or grow densely and cut young leaves as a spinach-like green or microgreen. This heat- and drought-tolerant heirloom shrugs off poor soil, producing tall stalks topped with feathery green seed heads that mature to gluten-free grain. Harvest seed when heads shatter easily and dry thoroughly before storing.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.

directions: Sow red amaranth directly once soil has warmed, scattering the fine seed on the surface and pressing in lightly. Plants climb up to six feet, crowned with dramatic burgundy seed heads that double as an ornamental accent and a nutritious grain harvest. Young leaves make an excellent cooked green, so pinch some early for the kitchen. Give it full sun and lean, well-drained soil, and it will thrive with little water.

planting.note: Sow in late summer or early fall for overwintering; a cold-hardy legume.

directions: Broadcast hairy vetch in late summer or early fall and cover about an inch deep so it can root before winter. This vigorous, cold-hardy legume sprawls across the soil, fixing large amounts of nitrogen and forming a weed-suppressing mat. It pairs especially well with cereal rye, which gives the vine something to climb. Terminate it at early bloom the following spring for a rich nitrogen boost before planting summer crops.

planting.note: Sow in late spring after soil warms; slow to establish but long-lived.

directions: Sow Indiangrass on prepared bare soil in late spring, pressing the fluffy seed in shallowly and keeping it moist during the slow germination period. This warm-season prairie perennial forms tall, upright clumps topped with shimmering golden seed heads that glow in autumn light. It is deeply rooted and drought-tolerant once established, excellent for erosion control and wildlife cover. Patience pays off, as stands strengthen considerably in their second and third seasons.

planting.note: Sow late spring into warm soil; a slow-to-establish native bunchgrass.

directions: Sow little bluestem on bare, firmed soil in late spring, barely covering the fluffy seed and keeping it lightly moist while it germinates. This warm-season native bunchgrass forms tidy clumps of fine blue-green blades that turn brilliant copper-red in fall and persist through winter. It thrives in lean, well-drained soil and needs little water once rooted, making it ideal for erosion control and butterfly habitat. Cut old growth back in late winter to make way for fresh spring blades.

planting.note: Sow early spring or late summer for a quick cool-season cover.

directions: Broadcast oat seed over worked soil in early spring or six to eight weeks before your first fall frost and rake in about half an inch. Fast, dense growth shades out weeds and its fibrous roots hold soil against erosion and winter rains. In cold zones the crop reliably winter-kills, leaving a soft mulch that is easy to plant into come spring. It also builds plenty of organic matter when turned under while still green.

planting.note: Sow late spring in warm soil; a deep-rooted native perennial grass.

directions: Sow switchgrass on bare soil in late spring once it has warmed, pressing the seed in shallowly and keeping it moist during its unhurried germination. This warm-season perennial forms upright clumps three to six feet tall with airy red-purple seed heads that catch the light and persist into winter. Its deep roots make it exceptionally drought-tolerant and superb for erosion control and wildlife cover. Leave standing stems through winter for interest, then cut back in early spring.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Sow African flake daisy directly after the last frost, barely covering the seed in a sunny, well-drained bed. These cheerful yellow blooms with purple centers open on compact, fine-foliaged plants that shrug off heat and drought. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the display going through the warm months. Perfect for water-wise borders and containers, it asks for little more than sun and sharp drainage.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Direct sow Autumn Beauty sunflowers an inch deep after the last frost, spacing them a foot or more apart for their branching habit. Each plant sends up multiple stems glowing in gold, bronze, orange, and burgundy, reaching five to seven feet tall from midsummer into fall. Their pollen-rich faces draw bees and butterflies, and the blooms make long-lasting cut flowers. Stake in windy sites and leave a few heads to ripen for the birds.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; calendula prefers cool weather.

directions: Sow Ball's Orange calendula a quarter inch deep around your last frost date, as it germinates best in cool soil. This heirloom pot marigold bears fully double, brilliant orange blooms on sturdy stems, both edible and prized for herbal salves. Deadhead regularly to prolong flowering, and pick petals for teas or garnishes. It reseeds happily and often gives a second flush as temperatures cool in fall.

planting.note: Sow in spring; seeds benefit from cold stratification, or fall sow.

directions: Surface-sow wild bergamot in spring after a period of cold, moist stratification, or sow outdoors in fall to let winter do the chilling. This native perennial in the mint family spreads into clumps of aromatic foliage topped by lavender, tufted blooms that hum with bees and hummingbirds. Give it good airflow to reduce powdery mildew, and deadhead to encourage repeat flowering. Deer and drought both leave it largely alone once established.

planting.note: Sow in spring; press seed onto surface as light aids germination.

directions: Press black eyed Susan seed onto the soil surface in spring without covering, since light aids germination. This tough native produces golden petals around a chocolate-brown cone, blooming for months and drawing bees and butterflies. It tolerates heat, drought, and poor soil, and is generally left alone by deer. Deadhead for tidiness or leave late seed heads for goldfinches and self-seeding.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.

directions: Sow Black Russian sunflower seeds an inch deep after the last frost in a spot with full sun and room to grow tall. This heirloom giant towers eight to twelve feet with a single large golden face and dark center, producing abundant oil-rich seeds for roasting or bird feed. Give young plants steady water and stake in exposed sites to support the heavy heads. Let heads dry on the stalk, then cut and cure them before harvesting the seeds.

planting.note: Press seed onto the surface after frost; light aids germination.

directions: Press blanket flower seed onto firmed soil around the last frost, leaving it uncovered since light speeds germination. This heat-loving wildflower blazes with red, orange, and yellow daisy-like blooms all summer, thriving in sandy, lean soils where other plants sulk. It is exceptionally drought-tolerant and a magnet for bees and butterflies. Deadhead for continuous color, or leave a few heads to self-sow next season.

planting.note: Sow in spring or fall; seeds benefit from cold stratification.

directions: Sow blue columbine in fall or after cold-stratifying the seed, pressing it onto the surface since light helps it sprout. This hardy perennial wildflower nods with delicate blue-and-white spurred blooms that lure hummingbirds and bees in late spring. It appreciates partial shade and consistent moisture, making it a graceful choice for woodland edges and containers. Leave seed heads to self-sow, as plants are relatively short-lived but readily replace themselves.

planting.note: Fall sow or cold-stratify seed before spring planting.

directions: Sow blue vervain in fall so winter cold breaks its dormancy, or cold-stratify the seed before a spring sowing. This native perennial sends up tall, candelabra spikes of blue-violet flowers that feed a wide range of pollinators through summer. It naturally favors moist meadows, rain gardens, and pond edges, so keep the soil damp. Long valued in herbal traditions, it combines ecological benefit with striking vertical form.

planting.note: Direct sow around the last frost; borage resents transplanting.

directions: Direct sow borage about half an inch deep around the last frost, since its taproot dislikes being transplanted. This fast-growing Mediterranean herb produces edible, star-shaped blue flowers and cucumber-flavored leaves that are favorites of bees. It makes an excellent companion plant and self-sows freely once established. Pinch flowers for salads and drinks, and let a few go to seed for next year's volunteers.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost; thrives in heat and poor soil.

directions: Direct sow Bright Lights cosmos after the last frost, barely covering the seed in a sunny, well-drained spot. This heat- and drought-tolerant heirloom produces semi-double blooms in glowing shades of gold, orange, and scarlet that keep coming until frost. Lean soil actually encourages more flowers and less floppy growth, so hold back on fertilizer. Deadhead or cut for bouquets to fuel a nonstop pollinator show.

planting.note: Fall sow or cold-stratify 30 days before spring planting.

directions: Sow butterfly milkweed in fall or cold-stratify the seed for about a month before a spring sowing to break dormancy. This native perennial forms compact, bushy clumps crowned with vivid orange flower clusters that are a critical nectar and host resource for Monarchs. Its deep taproot makes it drought-tolerant but slow to establish and best left undisturbed once planted. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and avoid rich, wet soils.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost; prefers cool growing conditions.

directions: Sow Apricot Beauty calendula a quarter inch deep around the last frost, as cool soil suits germination. This heirloom pot marigold bears soft semi-double blooms in apricot, peach, and pale-gold tones on tidy plants. The edible, medicinal petals are lovely in salads and herbal preparations. Deadhead often to extend bloom, and expect a fresh flush as autumn cools.

planting.note: Sow around the last frost in cool soil.

directions: Sow Pacific Beauty calendula a quarter inch deep near your last frost date, favoring the cool weather this heirloom loves. It produces a cheerful daisy-like mix of gold, orange, and cream blooms on plants up to two feet tall. The petals are both edible and valued in herbal skin-care preparations. Deadhead regularly for a long season, and let a few blooms set seed to self-sow.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.

directions: Direct sow California Giants zinnias a quarter inch deep after the last frost once soil is reliably warm. This classic heirloom produces massive four-to-six-inch double blooms in a rainbow of colors on tall, sturdy stems ideal for cutting. Pinch young plants to encourage branching, and cut or deadhead often to keep flowers coming until frost. Give good airflow to limit powdery mildew, and enjoy the constant parade of butterflies.

planting.note: Sow directly in early spring or fall; resents transplanting.

directions: Direct sow California poppy in early spring or fall, pressing the fine seed onto the surface since it needs light and dislikes root disturbance. This native wildflower opens brilliant orange, cup-shaped blooms above ferny blue-green foliage on compact plants. It thrives in poor, well-drained soil and full sun, needing almost no water once established. Let it self-sow freely and it will return year after year as a cheerful drift.

planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost and transplant out after.

directions: Start cape daisy indoors six weeks before the last frost, covering the seed lightly, then transplant to a sunny, well-drained bed once frost danger has passed. It bears elegant white petals around rich purple centers over a long bloom season and is grown as a tender perennial in mild climates. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the display fresh through the warm months. It tolerates some drought but flowers best with steady, moderate moisture.

planting.note: Surface sow around the last frost; light aids germination.

directions: Sprinkle Carpet of Snow alyssum onto firmed soil around the last frost and press in without covering, as light aids germination. This low, spreading heirloom carpets beds and container edges with masses of tiny, honey-scented white flowers. It draws in bees and beneficial insects and blooms from spring into fall, pausing only in extreme heat. Shear lightly if it flags mid-season to spark a fresh flush of bloom.

planting.note: Start indoors well before the last frost; slow to reach bloom.

directions: Start Chabaud carnations indoors eight to ten weeks before the last frost, covering seed lightly, then harden off and transplant after frost danger passes. This heirloom Dianthus bears fragrant, fully double blooms in a rich mix of colors prized for cut arrangements. Give it full sun, well-drained soil, and support the tall flower stems. Deadhead to prolong the display, and in mild zones it will return as a short-lived perennial.

planting.note: Sow after the last frost in warm soil.

directions: Direct sow Chocolate Cherry sunflowers an inch deep after the last frost, spacing them to allow for their branching habit. Each plant carries multiple five-to-eight-inch blooms in deep burgundy-to-black petals around dark chocolate centers on six-to-seven-foot stems. They make dramatic, pollen-rich cut flowers that keep coming through summer and fall. Cut regularly to encourage more side blooms and leave a few for the pollinators.

planting.note: Fall sow or early spring; biennial that blooms in its second year.

directions: Surface sow common evening primrose in fall or early spring, pressing the seed in lightly since it needs light to germinate. This hardy native biennial forms a low rosette its first year, then sends up tall stems of fragrant yellow flowers that open at dusk the second season. It thrives in poor, dry soil and tolerates neglect, making it ideal for tough spots and pollinator plantings. Let it self-sow to keep the two-year cycle going in the garden.

planting.note: Fall sow or cold-stratify 30 days before spring planting.

directions: Sow common milkweed in fall or cold-stratify the seed for a month before spring planting to break dormancy. This vigorous native perennial grows two to three feet tall with broad leaves and fragrant clusters of pink star-shaped flowers that are vital to Monarch butterflies. It spreads by rhizomes and can colonize, so site it where it has room to naturalize. Give it full sun and average soil, and it will host caterpillars and feed countless pollinators.
